title: The Daghdha
summary: Father god of the Celtic pantheon, master of weather and crops who wields an enormous club and possesses a bottomless cauldron.
content:
## The Daghdha

**Title:** The Good God, Father of All
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Nature, Trickery
**Symbol:** Bubbling cauldron or shield
**Pantheon:** Celtic

The Daghdha is the leader of the Celtic pantheon and one of its most powerful members. He is a boisterous, earthy god associated with weather, crops, and fertility. The Daghdha carries an enormous magical club that can slay the living with one end and restore the dead with the other. His cauldron, the Undry, never runs empty, symbolizing abundance and generosity. He is also the keeper of a magical harp that controls the seasons.

In a D&D campaign, The Daghdha appeals to druids, rangers, and any character tied to the land and its bounty. His clergy celebrate the turning of seasons and ensure bountiful harvests. Worshipers tend to be jovial and generous, reflecting their patron's love of feasting and merriment. Clerics of The Daghdha often serve as mediators in rural communities and guardians of sacred groves.

title: Arawn
summary: Celtic god of life and death, ruler of the otherworld realm of Annwn where the dead are judged.
content:
## Arawn

**Title:** Lord of Annwn, King of the Otherworld
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil
**Domains:** Life, Death
**Symbol:** Black star on gray background
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Arawn is the Celtic god who rules over Annwn, the otherworld realm where the dead are received and judged. Despite his evil alignment, Arawn is not a wantonly destructive deity; he is a stern and implacable judge who ensures that the cycle of life and death proceeds in its proper order. He keeps powerful hounds, the Cwn Annwn, whose eerie howling presages death.

In a D&D campaign, Arawn's followers include necromancers, those who tend to the dead, and warriors who seek power over life and death. His clerics oversee funerary rites and guard the boundary between the living and the dead. Some serve as executioners or judges, delivering Arawn's cold justice. Those who worship him do not necessarily seek to cause death, but they accept it as a natural and necessary force.

title: Belenus
summary: Celtic god of sun, light, and warmth, whose radiance drives away darkness and heals the sick.
content:
## Belenus

**Title:** The Shining One
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Light
**Symbol:** Solar disk and standing stones
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Belenus is the Celtic sun god, associated with light, warmth, and the healing power of the sun. He rides across the sky in a chariot pulled by blazing horses, driving back the forces of darkness and cold. The festival of Beltane, held at the start of summer, is named in his honor and features bonfires lit to celebrate his life-giving radiance.

In a D&D campaign, Belenus is worshiped by healers, farmers, and those who fight against undead and creatures of darkness. His clerics channel radiant energy and serve as beacons of hope in dark times. Temples to Belenus are often open-air circles of standing stones positioned to catch the sunrise. Adventurers who follow Belenus are drawn to quests that involve purging corruption and bringing light to shadowed places.

title: Brigantia
summary: Celtic goddess of rivers and livestock, protector of pastoral communities and flowing waters.
content:
## Brigantia

**Title:** Goddess of Rivers and Livestock
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life
**Symbol:** Footbridge
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Brigantia is the Celtic goddess of rivers, livestock, and the prosperity they bring to communities. She is the patron of pastoral life, watching over herders, their flocks, and the waterways that sustain the land. Rivers are considered sacred to her, and offerings are often cast into flowing water to earn her favor. She is a nurturing deity who ensures that the animals under her protection remain healthy and fertile.

In a D&D campaign, Brigantia's followers include herders, farmers, and those who live along rivers and streams. Her clerics serve rural communities, blessing livestock and ensuring clean water. They often act as mediators in disputes over grazing rights and water access. Adventurers who worship Brigantia may be drawn to protect farming communities from threats or to cleanse polluted waterways.

title: Diancecht
summary: Celtic god of medicine and healing, the divine physician who can restore even the most grievous wounds.
content:
## Diancecht

**Title:** The Divine Physician
**Alignment:** Lawful Good
**Domains:** Life
**Symbol:** Crossed oak and mistletoe branches
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Diancecht is the Celtic god of medicine and healing, renowned for his ability to cure any wound or illness. He once fashioned a silver hand for the war god Nuada after the original was lost in battle, demonstrating both his healing arts and his skill as a craftsman. Diancecht guards the Well of Slaine, whose waters can restore the dead to life, and he is fiercely protective of its secrets.

In a D&D campaign, Diancecht is the patron of healers, physicians, and herbalists. His clerics maintain hospitals and healing houses, tending to the sick and injured without regard for payment. They study both magical and mundane medicine, believing that knowledge of the body is sacred. Adventurers devoted to Diancecht often serve as party healers and may seek out rare medicinal herbs or lost healing knowledge.

title: Dunatis
summary: Celtic god of mountains and peaks, a stoic deity who embodies the enduring strength of stone.
content:
## Dunatis

**Title:** Lord of Mountains
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Nature
**Symbol:** Red sun-capped mountain peak
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Dunatis is the Celtic god of mountains and high places. He embodies the timeless, unyielding strength of stone and the majesty of towering peaks. Dunatis is a quiet, contemplative deity who speaks rarely but whose words carry the weight of mountains. He watches over those who dwell in highland regions and protects the sacred peaks where the mortal world draws closest to the divine.

In a D&D campaign, Dunatis appeals to druids, rangers, and monks who seek solitude and strength in mountainous terrain. His followers build shrines at the summits of peaks and meditate on the endurance of stone. Clerics of Dunatis often serve as guides through treacherous mountain passes and as protectors of alpine communities. Adventurers who follow him are drawn to exploring underground caverns and scaling the highest summits.

title: Goibhniu
summary: Celtic god of smiths and healing, a master craftsman who forges magical weapons and brews the ale of immortality.
content:
## Goibhniu

**Title:** The Great Smith
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Knowledge, Life
**Symbol:** Giant mallet over sword
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Goibhniu is the Celtic god of smithing and healing, one of the three craft gods known as the Tri De Dana. He forges weapons of supernatural quality that never miss their mark and always strike true. Goibhniu also brews a magical ale that grants immortality to the gods, combining his mastery of fire and forge with knowledge of healing and life-sustaining arts.

In a D&D campaign, Goibhniu is worshiped by smiths, armorers, and craftspeople of all kinds. His clerics maintain forges as sacred spaces and believe that the act of creation is itself a form of prayer. They often serve as healers as well, seeing the mending of flesh as akin to the mending of metal. Adventurers who follow Goibhniu seek out rare materials for crafting and may quest to recover lost forging techniques.

title: Lugh
summary: Celtic god of arts, travel, and commerce, known as the Many-Skilled for his mastery of every craft and discipline.
content:
## Lugh

**Title:** The Long-Handed, The Many-Skilled
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Knowledge, Life
**Symbol:** Pair of long hands
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Lugh is the Celtic god of arts, travel, and commerce, famed as Samildanach — the one skilled in all arts simultaneously. He earned his place among the gods by demonstrating that while each god mastered one skill, he alone mastered them all. Lugh wields the legendary Spear of Assal, which never misses, and he is associated with the harvest festival of Lughnasadh.

In a D&D campaign, Lugh appeals to bards, rogues, and any jack-of-all-trades character. His followers value versatility and the pursuit of excellence across multiple disciplines. Clerics of Lugh encourage trade, artistic expression, and safe travel between communities. Adventurers who worship Lugh are often well-rounded characters who pride themselves on having a skill for every situation.

title: Manannan mac Lir
summary: Celtic god of oceans and sea creatures, a mysterious mariner who guides souls to the otherworld across the waves.
content:
## Manannan mac Lir

**Title:** Lord of the Sea, Son of the Sea
**Alignment:** Lawful Neutral
**Domains:** Nature, Tempest
**Symbol:** Wave of white water on green
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Manannan mac Lir is the Celtic god of the sea and all its creatures. He rides the waves in a chariot pulled by seahorses and wears a cloak that shifts color like the ocean. Manannan is also a psychopomp, ferrying the worthy dead across the sea to the Blessed Isles. He possesses many magical treasures, including a boat that needs no sail and a sword that cuts through any armor.

In a D&D campaign, Manannan mac Lir is worshiped by sailors, fishers, and coastal communities. His clerics bless voyages, calm storms for the faithful, and perform funeral rites that commit the dead to the sea. They serve as navigators and protectors of harbors. Adventurers devoted to Manannan are drawn to oceanic quests, underwater exploration, and protecting coastal settlements from sea monsters and pirates.

metadata_json.symbol: Wave of white water on green

title: Math Mathonwy
summary: Celtic god of sorcery, a powerful and enigmatic wizard-king who commands arcane forces beyond mortal understanding.
content:
## Math Mathonwy

**Title:** The Ancient Sorcerer
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil
**Domains:** Knowledge
**Symbol:** Staff
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Math Mathonwy is the Celtic god of sorcery and arcane power. He is depicted as an ancient, inscrutable figure whose mastery of magic is absolute and whose motivations are often unknowable. Math can hear any whisper carried on the wind and possesses the ability to transform beings into animals or other forms as punishment or reward. His knowledge of the arcane arts is unmatched even among the gods.

In a D&D campaign, Math Mathonwy attracts wizards, sorcerers, and warlocks who seek arcane knowledge regardless of the moral cost. His followers believe that magical power transcends concepts of good and evil, and that true understanding requires exploring all aspects of the Art. Clerics of Math maintain hidden libraries of forbidden lore and may serve as patrons for warlocks. Adventurers who follow him pursue rare spells, magical artifacts, and ancient arcane secrets.

title: Morrigan
summary: Celtic goddess of battle, a fearsome war deity who appears as a crow over battlefields to claim the souls of the fallen.
content:
## Morrigan

**Title:** The Phantom Queen, The Crow of Battle
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** Two crossed spears
**Pantheon:** Celtic

The Morrigan is the Celtic goddess of battle, strife, and sovereignty. She appears on battlefields as a crow or raven, shrieking prophecies of doom and choosing which warriors will fall. The Morrigan is a shapeshifter who can appear as a beautiful woman, an old hag, or a trio of war goddesses. She revels in the chaos and carnage of combat, inciting conflicts and testing warriors to find those worthy of glory.

In a D&D campaign, the Morrigan is worshiped by warriors, barbarians, and those who embrace the chaos of battle. Her clerics are often found on the front lines of combat, channeling her fury and marking enemies for death. They perform auguries before battles and collect trophies from the fallen. Adventurers devoted to the Morrigan seek out combat and glory, believing that a death in battle is the highest honor.

title: Nuada
summary: Celtic god of war and warriors, the silver-handed king who leads the Tuatha De Danann in battle.
content:
## Nuada

**Title:** Silver Hand, King of the Tuatha De Danann
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** Silver hand on black background
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Nuada is the Celtic god of war and warriors, and the king of the Tuatha De Danann. He lost his hand in the First Battle of Mag Tuired and was fitted with a silver replacement by Diancecht, earning him the epithet Silver Hand. Nuada wields the Sword of Light, one of the four treasures of the Tuatha De Danann, from which no enemy can escape once it is drawn.

In a D&D campaign, Nuada is the patron of honorable warriors, military commanders, and those who lead through personal courage. Unlike the Morrigan, Nuada represents disciplined, purposeful warfare rather than chaotic bloodshed. His clerics train warriors, maintain armories, and serve as military advisors. Adventurers who follow Nuada value martial skill, leadership, and the protection of their people through strength of arms.

title: Oghma
summary: Celtic god of speech and writing, inventor of the Ogham alphabet and patron of eloquence and literacy.
content:
## Oghma

**Title:** The Honey-Mouthed, Lord of Knowledge
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Knowledge
**Symbol:** Unfurled scroll
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Oghma is the Celtic god of speech, writing, and eloquence. He is credited with inventing the Ogham script, the sacred alphabet of the Celtic peoples, and is revered as the patron of poets, scholars, and all who value the power of the written and spoken word. Oghma is depicted as a strong, handsome figure whose golden chains of eloquence link his tongue to the ears of willing listeners.

In a D&D campaign, Oghma appeals to bards, wizards, and any character who values knowledge and communication. His clerics maintain libraries, teach literacy, and serve as diplomats and negotiators. They believe that knowledge shared freely strengthens all of civilization. Adventurers who worship Oghma seek out lost texts, ancient inscriptions, and forgotten languages, believing that every piece of recovered knowledge enriches the world.

title: Silvanus
summary: Celtic god of nature and forests, the ancient protector of wild places who maintains the balance of the natural world.
content:
## Silvanus

**Title:** The Forest Father, the Old Oak
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Nature
**Symbol:** Summer oak tree
**Pantheon:** Celtic

Silvanus is the Celtic god of nature, forests, and the wild. He is one of the oldest deities in the pantheon, embodying the untamed power and quiet majesty of the natural world. Silvanus maintains the balance between civilization and wilderness, ensuring that neither encroaches too far upon the other. He appears as a weathered, ancient figure wreathed in leaves and bark, carrying a great oaken staff.

In a D&D campaign, Silvanus is the primary deity of druids and rangers in the Celtic tradition. His clerics patrol the borders between settled lands and wilderness, protecting sacred groves and ancient forests from despoilers. They mediate between loggers and the wild, seeking sustainable balance. Adventurers who follow Silvanus are guardians of the natural order, opposing those who would corrupt or destroy the wilderness.

title: Zeus
summary: King of the Greek gods, ruler of the sky and storms who enforces justice from his throne atop Mount Olympus.
content:
## Zeus

**Title:** King of the Gods, Lord of the Sky
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Tempest
**Symbol:** Fist full of lightning bolts
**Pantheon:** Greek

Zeus is the king of the Olympian gods and ruler of the sky, storms, and thunder. From his throne on Mount Olympus, he governs both gods and mortals, wielding devastating lightning bolts forged by the Cyclopes. Zeus is the arbiter of justice among the gods and the enforcer of sacred oaths, though he is also known for his many affairs and the complications they create.

In a D&D campaign, Zeus is worshiped by rulers, judges, and those who seek to wield authority justly. His clerics often serve in positions of civic leadership or as advisors to kings. They invoke his storms against oath-breakers and tyrants. Adventurers who follow Zeus are drawn to quests involving justice, governance, and the balance of power, often serving as champions of order against chaos.

title: Aphrodite
summary: Greek goddess of love and beauty, whose divine radiance can inspire passion or devastating jealousy.
content:
## Aphrodite

**Title:** Goddess of Love and Beauty
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Light
**Symbol:** Sea shell
**Pantheon:** Greek

Aphrodite is the Greek goddess of love, beauty, and desire. Born from the sea foam, she is the most beautiful of the Olympian gods, and her power over the hearts of mortals and immortals alike is absolute. Aphrodite can inspire the deepest love or the most destructive obsession with a glance. Despite her chaotic nature, she generally uses her power to bring joy and connection rather than suffering.

In a D&D campaign, Aphrodite is worshiped by lovers, artists, and those who value beauty in all its forms. Her clerics serve as matchmakers, counselors, and patrons of the arts. They maintain temples that double as sanctuaries for those seeking guidance in matters of the heart. Adventurers who follow Aphrodite may be diplomats who resolve conflicts through charm rather than force, or champions who defend love and beauty against those who would corrupt them.

title: Apollo
summary: Greek god of light, music, and healing, the radiant archer who drives the sun chariot and inspires prophecy.
content:
## Apollo

**Title:** The Radiant One, Lord of Light
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Knowledge, Life, Light
**Symbol:** Lyre
**Pantheon:** Greek

Apollo is the Greek god of light, music, poetry, healing, and prophecy. He drives the sun chariot across the sky each day and is the patron of the Oracle at Delphi. Apollo is one of the most widely worshiped of the Olympian gods, representing the ideals of beauty, harmony, and intellectual pursuit. He wields a silver bow with unerring aim and his music can soothe even the most savage beast.

In a D&D campaign, Apollo appeals to bards, healers, and seekers of knowledge. His clerics serve as oracles, musicians, and physicians, maintaining temples that combine healing houses with schools of art and music. They believe that beauty, health, and truth are interconnected aspects of divine light. Adventurers who follow Apollo often seek to uncover hidden truths, heal the afflicted, and bring the light of civilization to dark places.

title: Ares
summary: Greek god of war and strife, a brutal deity who revels in bloodshed and the chaos of the battlefield.
content:
## Ares

**Title:** The Warmaker, Lord of Battle
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** Spear
**Pantheon:** Greek

Ares is the Greek god of war, bloodshed, and violence. Unlike Athena, who represents the strategic and disciplined aspects of warfare, Ares embodies its brutal, savage, and chaotic nature. He is feared rather than loved, even by the other Olympian gods, who find his bloodlust distasteful. Ares delights in the carnage of battle for its own sake, favoring neither side but simply reveling in destruction.

In a D&D campaign, Ares is worshiped by berserkers, mercenaries, and those who fight for the sheer love of combat. His clerics incite conflicts and serve as war priests who charge into battle alongside their warriors. They perform blood sacrifices before combat and believe that strength in arms is the only true measure of worth. Adventurers who follow Ares are typically fighters and barbarians who seek glory through violence and conquest.

title: Artemis
summary: Greek goddess of hunting and childbirth, the wild huntress who protects the young and roams the moonlit forests.
content:
## Artemis

**Title:** The Huntress, Lady of the Wild
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life, Nature
**Symbol:** Bow and arrow on lunar disk
**Pantheon:** Greek

Artemis is the Greek goddess of the hunt, wilderness, wild animals, and childbirth. Twin sister of Apollo, she is a fierce and independent deity who roams the forests with her band of huntresses, shunning the company of men. Artemis is the protector of young women and children, and she is known for her swift and terrible vengeance against those who threaten the innocent or despoil the wilderness.

In a D&D campaign, Artemis appeals to rangers, druids, and any character who values the wilderness and independence. Her clerics protect wild places and the creatures that dwell within them, and they assist with childbirth and the protection of young. Adventurers devoted to Artemis are skilled hunters and trackers who defend the natural world against corruption and exploitation.

metadata_json.symbol: Bow and arrow on lunar disk

title: Athena
summary: Greek goddess of wisdom and craft, the brilliant strategist who champions civilization and just warfare.
content:
## Athena

**Title:** Goddess of Wisdom, The Gray-Eyed
**Alignment:** Lawful Good
**Domains:** Knowledge, War
**Symbol:** Owl
**Pantheon:** Greek

Athena is the Greek goddess of wisdom, craft, and strategic warfare. Born fully armored from the head of Zeus, she represents the intellectual and disciplined aspects of combat, in stark contrast to the brutish Ares. Athena is the patron of heroes, guiding them with wisdom and tactical acumen. She is also the goddess of skilled crafts, particularly weaving, and the protector of cities and civilization.

In a D&D campaign, Athena is worshiped by strategists, scholars, and fighters who value brains as much as brawn. Her clerics serve as advisors, teachers, and military strategists, emphasizing preparation and intelligence over brute force. Temples to Athena often house both libraries and training grounds. Adventurers who follow Athena approach challenges methodically and seek to protect civilization through wisdom and measured application of force.

title: Demeter
summary: Greek goddess of agriculture, the earth mother whose grief and joy govern the changing of the seasons.
content:
## Demeter

**Title:** The Earth Mother, Lady of the Harvest
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life
**Symbol:** Mare's head
**Pantheon:** Greek

Demeter is the Greek goddess of agriculture, harvest, and fertility of the earth. She is responsible for the growth of all crops and the turning of the seasons — when her daughter Persephone descends to the underworld, Demeter's grief causes winter, and her joy at Persephone's return brings spring. Demeter is a nurturing but powerful deity whose wrath can bring famine to entire nations.

In a D&D campaign, Demeter is worshiped by farmers, druids, and all who depend upon the land for sustenance. Her clerics bless fields, predict weather patterns, and ensure bountiful harvests. They perform seasonal rituals marking planting and harvest times. Adventurers who follow Demeter are drawn to protecting agricultural communities from threats such as blights, drought, and marauding monsters that threaten the food supply.

title: Dionysus
summary: Greek god of mirth and wine, the ecstatic deity who blurs the boundaries between joy and madness.
content:
## Dionysus

**Title:** The Twice-Born, God of Wine and Revelry
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Life
**Symbol:** Thyrsus (staff tipped with pine cone)
**Pantheon:** Greek

Dionysus is the Greek god of wine, mirth, festivity, and ecstasy. He represents the wild, untamed aspects of nature and the liberation that comes from abandoning social restraints. Dionysus is both a giver of joy and a bringer of madness — his revelries can inspire great art and communion, or drive participants to destructive frenzy. He is the patron of theater and the dramatic arts.

In a D&D campaign, Dionysus appeals to bards, revelers, and free spirits who reject rigid social structures. His clerics organize festivals, maintain vineyards, and serve as performers and entertainers. They believe that joy and celebration are sacred acts that bring mortals closer to the divine. Adventurers who follow Dionysus are often charismatic and unpredictable, embracing life with passionate abandon.

title: Hades
summary: Greek god of the underworld, the grim ruler of the dead who hoards both souls and the treasures beneath the earth.
content:
## Hades

**Title:** Lord of the Underworld, The Unseen One
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil
**Domains:** Death
**Symbol:** Black ram
**Pantheon:** Greek

Hades is the Greek god of the underworld and the dead. He rules over the vast realm beneath the earth where all mortal souls eventually come to reside. Hades is stern, unyielding, and utterly implacable — once a soul enters his domain, it never leaves. He is also the god of the wealth found beneath the earth, including precious metals and gems. Unlike many death gods, Hades is not actively malicious, but his domain and his unwillingness to show mercy earn him his evil alignment.

In a D&D campaign, Hades is worshiped by those who deal with the dead, including undertakers, necromancers, and those who seek the treasures of the earth. His clerics oversee funeral rites and guard against the undead, which represent an affront to Hades' orderly dominion over the dead. Adventurers who follow Hades may delve into dungeons seeking buried wealth or quest to return escaped souls to the underworld.

title: Hecate
summary: Greek goddess of magic and crossroads, a mysterious triple-faced deity who holds power over sorcery and the night.
content:
## Hecate

**Title:** Queen of Witches, Goddess of Crossroads
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil
**Domains:** Knowledge, Trickery
**Symbol:** Setting moon
**Pantheon:** Greek

Hecate is the Greek goddess of magic, crossroads, and the night. She is a liminal deity who dwells at the boundaries between worlds — the crossroads where paths diverge, the threshold between life and death, and the border between the mortal realm and the supernatural. Hecate holds dominion over sorcery, witchcraft, and necromancy, and she is often depicted as a triple-formed figure holding torches and keys.

In a D&D campaign, Hecate is worshiped by witches, warlocks, and practitioners of dark magic. Her clerics maintain shrines at crossroads and perform rituals under the new moon. They serve as intermediaries between the mortal world and darker powers, offering guidance to those who seek forbidden knowledge. Adventurers who follow Hecate are drawn to mysteries, arcane secrets, and the exploration of liminal spaces between planes of existence.

title: Hephaestus
summary: Greek god of smithing and craft, the divine artisan who forges wondrous items in his volcanic workshop.
content:
## Hephaestus

**Title:** The Divine Smith, Lord of the Forge
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Knowledge
**Symbol:** Hammer and anvil
**Pantheon:** Greek

Hephaestus is the Greek god of fire, metalworking, and craftsmanship. Despite being cast from Olympus for his lameness, he became the greatest artisan among the gods, forging Zeus's thunderbolts, Achilles' armor, and countless other marvels in his volcanic forge. Hephaestus represents the dignity of honest labor and the power of creation, proving that worth is measured by what one creates rather than by appearance.

In a D&D campaign, Hephaestus is the patron of smiths, engineers, and inventors of all kinds. His clerics maintain forges as sacred spaces and teach the craft of metalworking as a form of devotion. They believe that creating something useful and beautiful is the highest form of worship. Adventurers who follow Hephaestus seek rare materials, lost crafting techniques, and opportunities to create items of lasting value.

title: Hera
summary: Greek goddess of marriage and intrigue, the queen of Olympus who fiercely protects the sanctity of marriage vows.
content:
## Hera

**Title:** Queen of the Gods, Protector of Marriage
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Trickery
**Symbol:** Fan of peacock feathers
**Pantheon:** Greek

Hera is the Greek goddess of marriage, women, and family, and the queen of the Olympian gods. She is a proud and powerful deity who fiercely guards the institution of marriage. Hera is known for her cunning schemes and her terrible vengeance against those who violate their marriage vows or threaten her position. Despite her chaotic tendencies, she represents the binding power of oaths and commitments.

In a D&D campaign, Hera is worshiped by married couples, nobility, and those who value family bonds and political alliances. Her clerics officiate marriages, counsel troubled couples, and exact punishment on oath-breakers. They are skilled in intrigue and court politics. Adventurers who follow Hera may be drawn to political quests, defending the honor of wronged spouses, or navigating the complex webs of noble intrigue.

title: Hercules
summary: Greek god of strength and adventure, the legendary hero who ascended to godhood through his twelve labors.
content:
## Hercules

**Title:** The Mighty, Champion of the Gods
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Tempest, War
**Symbol:** Lion's head
**Pantheon:** Greek

Hercules is the Greek god of strength, heroes, and adventure. Originally a mortal demigod, he earned his place among the Olympians through his legendary Twelve Labors and countless other heroic deeds. Hercules represents the triumph of courage and determination over seemingly impossible odds. He is the patron of athletes, warriors, and anyone who faces overwhelming challenges with strength and bravery.

In a D&D campaign, Hercules is worshiped by fighters, barbarians, and adventurers who admire physical prowess and heroic deeds. His clerics encourage feats of strength and courage, organize athletic competitions, and chronicle the deeds of heroes. They believe that mortal heroes can achieve the impossible through determination and divine favor. Adventurers who follow Hercules seek out legendary challenges and impossible quests, aspiring to match the labors of their patron.

title: Hermes
summary: Greek god of travel and commerce, the swift messenger of the gods who also guides souls to the underworld.
content:
## Hermes

**Title:** The Messenger, Guide of Souls
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Trickery
**Symbol:** Caduceus (winged staff and serpents)
**Pantheon:** Greek

Hermes is the Greek god of travel, commerce, thieves, and communication. He serves as the messenger of the gods, darting between Olympus, the mortal world, and the underworld with supernatural speed thanks to his winged sandals. Hermes is also a psychopomp who guides the souls of the dead to the underworld, and the patron of merchants, travelers, and clever rogues. He is the most approachable of the Olympian gods, often intervening directly in mortal affairs.

In a D&D campaign, Hermes is worshiped by rogues, merchants, travelers, and bards. His clerics protect travelers, facilitate trade, and serve as messengers and diplomats. They maintain roadside shrines and wayside inns along major trade routes. Adventurers who follow Hermes value cleverness, quick thinking, and the freedom of the open road, preferring to outwit their enemies rather than overpower them.

title: Hestia
summary: Greek goddess of home and family, the gentle keeper of the sacred hearth fire that burns at the heart of every household.
content:
## Hestia

**Title:** Keeper of the Hearth
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life
**Symbol:** Hearth
**Pantheon:** Greek

Hestia is the Greek goddess of the hearth, home, and family. She is the eldest of the Olympian gods, yet the most modest and unassuming. Hestia tends the sacred hearth fire on Mount Olympus and is present in every household fire and every communal gathering place. She represents domestic harmony, hospitality, and the warmth of a welcoming home. Hestia chose to remain a virgin goddess, devoting herself entirely to her role as keeper of the flame.

In a D&D campaign, Hestia is worshiped by those who value home, family, and community. Her clerics tend public hearth fires, provide shelter to travelers, and work to strengthen community bonds. They believe that the hearth is sacred ground where all are welcome and disputes are set aside. Adventurers who follow Hestia may seem unlikely travelers, but they carry the warmth of home with them and strive to create safe havens wherever they go.

title: Nike
summary: Greek goddess of victory, the winged champion who bestows triumph upon the worthy in battle and competition.
content:
## Nike

**Title:** The Winged Victory
**Alignment:** Lawful Neutral
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** Winged woman
**Pantheon:** Greek

Nike is the Greek goddess of victory, both in battle and in peaceful competition. She flies across battlefields and arenas, bestowing her favor upon those she deems worthy of triumph. Nike is a constant companion of Zeus and Athena, and she represents the culmination of effort, skill, and determination. Her presence guarantees success, and warriors and athletes alike pray fervently for her blessing before any contest.

In a D&D campaign, Nike is worshiped by competitive warriors, athletes, and commanders who seek victory above all else. Her clerics bless armies before battle, organize tournaments, and maintain shrines where warriors come to pray for success. They believe that victory is earned through preparation and excellence, not merely luck. Adventurers who follow Nike are driven competitors who push themselves to excel in every challenge they face.

title: Pan
summary: Greek god of nature and shepherds, the goat-footed piper whose wild music inspires both joy and primal terror.
content:
## Pan

**Title:** Lord of the Wild, The Goat-Footed God
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Nature
**Symbol:** Syrinx (pan pipes)
**Pantheon:** Greek

Pan is the Greek god of the wild, shepherds, flocks, and rustic music. He appears as a satyr — half-man, half-goat — dwelling in forests and mountains far from civilization. Pan's haunting pipe music can inspire ecstasy or sheer terror; the word "panic" derives from the fear he can instill. He is a lusty, carefree deity who embodies the untamed aspects of nature and the simple joys of pastoral life.

In a D&D campaign, Pan is worshiped by shepherds, druids, satyrs, and those who embrace wild freedom. His clerics tend flocks, protect wilderness areas, and lead wild revels under the stars. They believe that civilization is an artificial construct that separates mortals from their true nature. Adventurers who follow Pan are often rangers or druids who feel most at home in untamed wilderness, using music and nature magic to overcome challenges.

title: Poseidon
summary: Greek god of the sea and earthquakes, the tempestuous earth-shaker who rules over all the world's waters.
content:
## Poseidon

**Title:** The Earth-Shaker, Lord of the Seas
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Tempest
**Symbol:** Trident
**Pantheon:** Greek

Poseidon is the Greek god of the sea, earthquakes, and horses. Brother of Zeus and Hades, he rules over the vast oceans and all the creatures within them. Poseidon is a temperamental and vengeful deity — when angered, he smashes the ground with his trident to cause earthquakes and tsunamis. When pleased, he calms the seas and grants safe passage. He created horses from the crashing waves and is their patron as well.

In a D&D campaign, Poseidon is worshiped by sailors, fishers, and anyone who depends on the sea for their livelihood. His clerics maintain temples in port cities and perform rituals to ensure safe voyages and calm seas. They also serve as intermediaries during natural disasters involving water or earthquakes. Adventurers who follow Poseidon are drawn to naval adventures, underwater exploration, and defending coastal communities from sea monsters and storms.

title: Tyche
summary: Greek goddess of good fortune, the unpredictable deity who governs luck, chance, and fate's turning wheel.
content:
## Tyche

**Title:** Lady Luck, Goddess of Fortune
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Trickery
**Symbol:** Red pentagram
**Pantheon:** Greek

Tyche is the Greek goddess of fortune, luck, and chance. She governs the unpredictable turns of fate that can elevate a pauper to a king or cast down the mighty. Tyche is depicted carrying a cornucopia of abundance in one hand and a rudder in the other, symbolizing her power to steer the course of mortal lives. She is notoriously fickle, favoring no one for long and delighting in the unexpected.

In a D&D campaign, Tyche is worshiped by gamblers, merchants, and adventurers who rely on luck and fortune. Her clerics maintain gambling houses and serve as fortune-tellers, teaching that fate is mutable and that fortune favors the bold. They believe that taking risks is a form of worship. Adventurers who follow Tyche embrace uncertainty and trust in luck, often making bold gambles that either pay off spectacularly or fail dramatically.

title: Re-Horakhty
summary: Supreme Egyptian sun god and creator deity, the falcon-headed lord whose radiance gives life to all things.
content:
## Re-Horakhty

**Title:** The Sun God, Horus of the Two Horizons
**Alignment:** Lawful Good
**Domains:** Life, Light
**Symbol:** Solar disk encircled by serpent
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Re-Horakhty is the supreme deity of the Egyptian pantheon, a fusion of the sun god Re and the sky god Horus. He represents the sun at its zenith, the ultimate source of life and light. Each day he sails across the sky in his solar barque, battling the serpent Apep through the underworld each night to ensure the sun rises again. Re-Horakhty is the creator of all things and the rightful ruler of gods and mortals alike.

In a D&D campaign, Re-Horakhty is worshiped by rulers, paladins, and all who champion light against darkness. His clerics serve as judges, healers, and guardians who channel the power of the sun to smite evil. They perform elaborate dawn rituals to celebrate the sun's daily triumph over darkness. Adventurers who follow Re-Horakhty are champions of righteousness who seek to destroy undead, fiends, and other creatures of darkness.

title: Anubis
summary: Egyptian jackal-headed god who judges the dead, weighing their hearts against the feather of truth.
content:
## Anubis

**Title:** Guardian of the Dead, The Jackal God
**Alignment:** Lawful Neutral
**Domains:** Death
**Symbol:** Black jackal
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Anubis is the Egyptian god of mummification, the afterlife, and the judgment of the dead. Depicted with the head of a jackal, he guides souls to the Hall of Judgment where he weighs their hearts against the feather of Ma'at. Those whose hearts are light pass on to the blessed afterlife; those found wanting are devoured. Anubis is a solemn and meticulous deity who takes his duties with absolute seriousness.

In a D&D campaign, Anubis is worshiped by morticians, tomb guardians, and those who seek just passage to the afterlife. His clerics embalm the dead, protect tombs from desecration, and hunt down those who disturb the rest of the departed. They are also fierce enemies of undead, which they view as abominations that defy the natural order of death. Adventurers who follow Anubis may serve as dungeon delvers who ensure the dead remain undisturbed.

title: Apep
summary: Egyptian serpent god of evil and fire, the great destroyer who eternally seeks to devour the sun and plunge the world into darkness.
content:
## Apep

**Title:** The Serpent of Chaos, The World Destroyer
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil
**Domains:** Trickery
**Symbol:** Flaming snake
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Apep is the Egyptian god of evil, fire, and chaos, manifesting as a colossal serpent that dwells in the underworld. Each night, Apep attacks the solar barque of Re-Horakhty as it passes through the underworld, attempting to swallow the sun and plunge the world into eternal darkness. He is the embodiment of all that opposes order, light, and creation — pure destructive chaos given serpentine form.

In a D&D campaign, Apep is worshiped by cultists, arsonists, and those who seek to overthrow the established order through destruction. His followers operate in secret, working to undermine civilization and spread chaos. Clerics of Apep use fire and poison as their primary weapons and ally with serpentine creatures. Adventurers rarely follow Apep, but his cults make excellent villains for campaigns centered on stopping catastrophic evil.

title: Bast
summary: Egyptian cat goddess of protection and vengeance, a fierce defender of the home who punishes those who threaten her charges.
content:
## Bast

**Title:** The Cat Goddess, Lady of the East
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** Cat
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Bast is the Egyptian cat goddess of protection, pleasure, and vengeance. Originally a fierce lioness war deity, she evolved into a more benevolent protector associated with domestic cats, joy, dance, and music. However, she retains her warrior aspect and is a ferocious defender of the innocent, particularly women and children. Bast's vengeance against those who threaten her charges is swift and merciless.

In a D&D campaign, Bast is worshiped by warriors, guardians, and those who protect homes and families. Her clerics serve as community defenders and avengers of wrongs committed against the vulnerable. They keep cats as sacred companions and believe that the animals carry Bast's watchful gaze. Adventurers who follow Bast are protective fighters who channel their goddess's fierce maternal instinct into righteous combat against those who prey upon the weak.

title: Bes
summary: Egyptian dwarf god of luck and music, a jovial protector of households who drives away evil spirits with dance and song.
content:
## Bes

**Title:** The Protector, The Dancing Dwarf
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Trickery
**Symbol:** Misshapen dwarf image
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Bes is the Egyptian god of luck, music, dance, and household protection. Unlike the regal, animal-headed forms of most Egyptian deities, Bes appears as a squat, bearded dwarf with a leonine mane. Despite his comical appearance, he is a fierce protector who drives away evil spirits with his wild dancing and loud music. Bes guards the home, protects childbirth, and brings good fortune to those he favors.

In a D&D campaign, Bes is worshiped by common folk, entertainers, and those who rely on luck and good humor to see them through. His clerics are jovial protectors who use music and merriment as both weapons and shields against evil. They ward homes against malicious spirits and celebrate life's simple joys. Adventurers who follow Bes are often bards or rogues with a cheerful disposition who use trickery and wit rather than brute force.

title: Hathor
summary: Egyptian goddess of love, music, and motherhood, the celestial cow whose gentle radiance nurtures all living things.
content:
## Hathor

**Title:** The Golden One, Mistress of Heaven
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life, Light
**Symbol:** Horned cow's head with lunar disk
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Hathor is the Egyptian goddess of love, beauty, music, joy, and motherhood. She is often depicted as a cow or as a woman with cow's horns cradling a solar disk. Hathor represents the nurturing, life-giving aspects of the divine feminine and is one of the most beloved deities in the Egyptian pantheon. She presides over celebrations, protects mothers and children, and welcomes the worthy dead to the afterlife with food and drink.

In a D&D campaign, Hathor is worshiped by artists, musicians, lovers, and mothers. Her clerics serve as midwives, counselors, and patrons of the arts, maintaining temples filled with music and beauty. They organize festivals, bless marriages, and comfort the dying with promises of a joyful afterlife. Adventurers who follow Hathor seek to spread joy and beauty in the world, defending communities and nurturing those in need.

metadata_json.symbol: Horned cow's head with lunar disk

title: Imhotep
summary: Egyptian god of crafts and medicine, the mortal architect and healer who was elevated to divine status for his wisdom.
content:
## Imhotep

**Title:** The God of Medicine, Master Builder
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Knowledge
**Symbol:** Step pyramid
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Imhotep is the Egyptian god of medicine, architecture, and craftsmanship. Uniquely among the Egyptian pantheon, Imhotep was originally a mortal — a brilliant architect, physician, and vizier who designed the first step pyramid. His achievements were so extraordinary that he was deified after death, becoming the patron of healers and builders. Imhotep represents the power of mortal ingenuity elevated to divine status.

In a D&D campaign, Imhotep is worshiped by architects, engineers, physicians, and scholars. His clerics build hospitals, design monuments, and advance the boundaries of knowledge through practical application. They believe that the greatest worship is to create something that endures and benefits others. Adventurers who follow Imhotep are drawn to exploring ancient ruins for architectural knowledge, recovering lost medical techniques, and building lasting structures.

title: Isis
summary: Egyptian goddess of fertility and magic, the great enchantress whose mastery of sorcery rivals that of any god.
content:
## Isis

**Title:** The Great Enchantress, Mother of Magic
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Knowledge, Life
**Symbol:** Ankh and star
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Isis is the Egyptian goddess of magic, fertility, and motherhood. She is considered the most powerful magician in the Egyptian pantheon, having tricked Re into revealing his secret name, which gave her power over all creation. Isis is a devoted mother and wife — she famously reassembled the body of her murdered husband Osiris and conceived their son Horus through her magical arts. She represents the protective power of maternal love combined with supreme arcane mastery.

In a D&D campaign, Isis is worshiped by wizards, sorcerers, and mothers. Her clerics serve as healers, protectors of families, and guardians of magical knowledge. They believe that magic is a gift to be used wisely for the protection and benefit of others. Adventurers who follow Isis combine magical prowess with a protective nature, seeking to recover lost magical knowledge and defend the vulnerable.

title: Nephthys
summary: Egyptian goddess of death and grief, the compassionate mourner who comforts the bereaved and guides the dead to peace.
content:
## Nephthys

**Title:** The Mourning Goddess, Friend of the Dead
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Death
**Symbol:** Horns around a lunar disk
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Nephthys is the Egyptian goddess of death, grief, lamentation, and the night. Sister of Isis and wife of Set, she chose to stand against her husband's evil and helped Isis recover and resurrect Osiris. Nephthys is a compassionate deity who comforts mourners, protects the dead, and ensures that funeral rites are properly observed. She represents the gentler, more sorrowful aspects of death — not its violence, but its inevitability and the grief it leaves behind.

In a D&D campaign, Nephthys is worshiped by mourners, undertakers, and those who tend to the dying. Her clerics perform funeral rites, comfort the bereaved, and protect the resting places of the dead. They oppose undead as perversions of the natural order and work to ease the passage of souls to the afterlife. Adventurers who follow Nephthys are compassionate souls who help others face death with dignity and who fight against those who would disturb the dead.

title: Osiris
summary: Egyptian god of nature and the underworld, the resurrected king who judges the dead and ensures the renewal of life.
content:
## Osiris

**Title:** Lord of the Dead, The Resurrected King
**Alignment:** Lawful Good
**Domains:** Life, Nature
**Symbol:** Crook and flail
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Osiris is the Egyptian god of the afterlife, the underworld, and the regeneration of life. He was murdered and dismembered by his brother Set, but was resurrected through the magic of his wife Isis, becoming the ruler of the underworld and judge of the dead. Osiris represents the eternal cycle of death and rebirth — just as crops die and are replanted, so too do souls pass through death to new life under his dominion.

In a D&D campaign, Osiris is worshiped by farmers, judges, and those who believe in redemption and the afterlife. His clerics tend to both the living and the dead, overseeing harvests and funeral rites with equal devotion. They teach that death is not an ending but a transformation. Adventurers who follow Osiris seek justice for the wronged, oppose the forces of Set, and work to ensure that the cycle of life and death remains unbroken.

title: Ptah
summary: Egyptian god of crafts and knowledge, the divine artisan who spoke the world into existence through the power of thought and word.
content:
## Ptah

**Title:** The Creator, Lord of Truth
**Alignment:** Lawful Neutral
**Domains:** Knowledge
**Symbol:** Bull
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Ptah is the Egyptian god of craftsmen, architects, and creation itself. According to his cult, Ptah created the world through the power of his heart (thought) and tongue (speech) — conceiving all things in his mind and then speaking them into existence. He is the patron of all artisans and the master of all crafts, from stonemasonry to metalworking. Ptah is a contemplative deity who values precision, skill, and the patient perfection of one's craft.

In a D&D campaign, Ptah is worshiped by artisans, architects, and artificers who seek to create works of lasting beauty and utility. His clerics maintain workshops as sacred spaces and teach that the act of creation is the highest form of worship. They believe that through craft, mortals participate in the divine act of shaping reality. Adventurers who follow Ptah seek rare materials, study ancient building techniques, and create items of extraordinary quality.

title: Set
summary: Egyptian god of darkness and desert storms, the treacherous brother of Osiris who embodies chaos, violence, and desolation.
content:
## Set

**Title:** Lord of the Desert, The Usurper
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil
**Domains:** Death, Tempest, Trickery
**Symbol:** Coiled cobra
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Set is the Egyptian god of darkness, desert storms, violence, and chaos. He is infamous for murdering his brother Osiris out of jealousy and ambition, an act that made him the eternal enemy of Horus and a symbol of treachery among the gods. Set commands the barren deserts, sandstorms, and the hostile creatures that dwell within them. He is a god of raw, destructive power who revels in disorder and conflict.

In a D&D campaign, Set is worshiped by evil cultists, desert raiders, and those who embrace chaos and destruction. His clerics spread discord, assassinate rivals, and perform dark rituals in hidden desert temples. They command serpents and scorpions, and they work to undermine the worship of other Egyptian deities. Adventurers rarely follow Set, but his cults provide dangerous antagonists for campaigns set in desert regions or involving ancient Egyptian-themed civilizations.

title: Sobek
summary: Egyptian crocodile god of water and military prowess, a fearsome predator deity who commands the rivers and marshes.
content:
## Sobek

**Title:** The Crocodile God, Lord of the Waters
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil
**Domains:** Nature, Tempest
**Symbol:** Crocodile head with horns and plumes
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Sobek is the Egyptian god of crocodiles, water, marshes, and military strength. He is depicted as a man with the head of a crocodile, embodying the terrifying power and primal fury of these apex predators. Sobek is both feared and revered — his power over the Nile's floods can bring either life-giving irrigation or devastating destruction. He is an aggressive, territorial deity who demands respect and offerings from those who enter his domain.

In a D&D campaign, Sobek is worshiped by warriors, crocodile-hunters, and those who live along rivers and in marshlands. His clerics maintain temples near rivers and swamps, keeping sacred crocodiles as living avatars of their god. They serve as enforcers and military leaders, channeling Sobek's predatory nature into disciplined aggression. Adventurers who follow Sobek are fierce combatants who dominate their territory and show no mercy to trespassers.

metadata_json.symbol: Crocodile head with horns and plumes

title: Thoth
summary: Egyptian ibis-headed god of knowledge and wisdom, the divine scribe who invented writing and maintains cosmic balance.
content:
## Thoth

**Title:** Lord of Knowledge, The Divine Scribe
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Knowledge
**Symbol:** Ibis
**Pantheon:** Egyptian

Thoth is the Egyptian god of knowledge, wisdom, writing, and the moon. He is the divine scribe who records the judgments of the dead and maintains the balance of the cosmos through his mastery of sacred mathematics and language. Thoth invented hieroglyphics and gifted writing to mortals, and he serves as arbiter and counselor among the gods. His wisdom is sought by all, and his judgments are considered final.

In a D&D campaign, Thoth is worshiped by scholars, scribes, wizards, and anyone who values knowledge above all else. His clerics maintain vast libraries and scribal schools, meticulously recording history, laws, and magical formulas. They believe that knowledge is the foundation of all power and that ignorance is the greatest threat to civilization. Adventurers who follow Thoth seek out ancient texts, lost libraries, and forgotten lore, cataloguing their discoveries for future generations.

title: Odin
summary: Allfather of the Norse gods, the one-eyed god of knowledge and war who sacrificed his eye at Mimir's Well for wisdom.
content:
## Odin

**Title:** The Allfather, Lord of Asgard
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Knowledge, War
**Symbol:** Watching blue eye
**Pantheon:** Norse

Odin is the king of the Norse gods and ruler of Asgard. He sacrificed his eye at Mimir's Well to gain cosmic wisdom and hung himself from Yggdrasil for nine days to learn the secrets of the runes. Odin is a complex deity — simultaneously a wise counselor and a cunning war god, a patron of poets and a lord of the slain. He sends his ravens, Huginn and Muninn, across the worlds each day to gather knowledge, and he gathers fallen warriors in Valhalla to prepare for Ragnarok.

In a D&D campaign, Odin is worshiped by warriors, sages, and rulers who seek both wisdom and martial prowess. His clerics serve as advisors, battle-priests, and keepers of runic lore. They value both intellectual pursuit and valor in combat, seeing no contradiction between the pen and the sword. Adventurers who follow Odin are driven to seek knowledge, prove themselves in battle, and prepare for the great conflicts to come.

title: Aegir
summary: Norse god of the sea and storms, a giant-born brewer of divine ale whose halls host feasts for the gods beneath the waves.
content:
## Aegir

**Title:** Lord of the Deep, The Ale-Brewer
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil
**Domains:** Tempest
**Symbol:** Rough ocean waves
**Pantheon:** Norse

Aegir is the Norse god of the sea, storms, and brewing. Though technically a giant rather than one of the Aesir, he is accepted among the gods and hosts legendary feasts in his underwater hall, illuminated by gleaming gold. Aegir's nine daughters personify the waves, and his cauldron brews the finest ale in all the Nine Worlds. However, Aegir is a dangerous deity — he capsizes ships to claim their crews and cargo, and sailors who fail to offer tribute risk his wrath.

In a D&D campaign, Aegir is worshiped by Viking sailors, fishermen, and brewers. His clerics perform sacrifices before sea voyages and maintain coastal shrines where offerings are cast into the waves. They serve as navigators and weather-readers, interpreting Aegir's moods through the state of the sea. Adventurers who follow Aegir are drawn to naval adventures, undersea exploration, and the acquisition of treasure from shipwrecks.

title: Balder
summary: Norse god of beauty and poetry, the beloved and radiant son of Odin whose death heralds the coming of Ragnarok.
content:
## Balder

**Title:** The Beautiful, The Shining One
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life, Light
**Symbol:** Gem-encrusted silver chalice
**Pantheon:** Norse

Balder is the Norse god of beauty, joy, purity, and poetry. He is the most beloved of the Aesir, radiant and fair beyond all others. Balder's death at the hands of Loki's trickery — struck by a mistletoe dart, the one thing in all creation that had not sworn to protect him — is one of the most tragic events in Norse mythology and a harbinger of Ragnarok. His eventual return after the world's renewal symbolizes hope and resurrection.

In a D&D campaign, Balder is worshiped by poets, artists, and those who seek to bring beauty and light into the world. His clerics are healers and peacemakers who work to prevent conflict and nurture joy in their communities. They view beauty and goodness as shields against the coming darkness. Adventurers who follow Balder seek to inspire hope, protect the innocent, and delay the forces of destruction that threaten to bring about the end times.

title: Bragi
summary: Norse god of poetry and music, the silver-tongued skald whose eloquence can move gods and mortals to tears or action.
content:
## Bragi

**Title:** God of Poetry, The Skald of the Gods
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Light
**Symbol:** Harp
**Pantheon:** Norse

Bragi is the Norse god of poetry, eloquence, and music. He is the foremost skald of the gods, whose tongue is inscribed with runes of power that give his words magical force. Bragi welcomes slain heroes to Valhalla with songs of their deeds, and his poetry inspires courage in warriors and wisdom in rulers. He is married to Idun, the keeper of the golden apples of immortality.

In a D&D campaign, Bragi is the patron of bards, poets, and storytellers. His clerics preserve the oral traditions of their people through saga and song, believing that the stories we tell shape the world as much as the deeds we perform. They serve as entertainers, historians, and diplomats whose words carry divine authority. Adventurers who follow Bragi chronicle the deeds of heroes and use the power of words and music to inspire allies and demoralize foes.

title: Forseti
summary: Norse god of justice and law, the impartial arbiter whose rulings are so fair that no one can dispute them.
content:
## Forseti

**Title:** The Lawgiver, God of Justice
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Light
**Symbol:** Head of a bearded man
**Pantheon:** Norse

Forseti is the Norse god of justice, law, and reconciliation. Son of Balder and Nanna, he presides over the hall of Glitnir, whose pillars are of gold and roof is of silver. Forseti is the greatest arbiter among the gods, and his judgments are so perfectly fair that no party can find fault with them. He settles disputes among both gods and mortals, and his word is considered final and binding.

In a D&D campaign, Forseti is worshiped by judges, lawmakers, and those who seek fair resolution to disputes. His clerics serve as arbiters, mediators, and lawgivers in their communities, settling conflicts with wisdom and impartiality. They maintain courts of law and believe that justice must be administered without bias or favoritism. Adventurers who follow Forseti are driven to right wrongs, expose corruption in legal systems, and ensure that justice prevails.

title: Frey
summary: Norse god of fertility and the sun, the generous lord of plenty who brings peace, prosperity, and good harvests.
content:
## Frey

**Title:** Lord of Plenty, God of the Sun
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life, Light
**Symbol:** Ice-blue greatsword
**Pantheon:** Norse

Frey is the Norse god of fertility, sunlight, peace, and prosperity. He is one of the Vanir gods, along with his sister Freya and father Njord, who came to live among the Aesir as part of a peace agreement. Frey possesses Skidbladnir, a ship that can carry all the gods yet fold to fit in a pocket, and once wielded a magic sword that fights on its own — which he gave away for love, a sacrifice that will cost him dearly at Ragnarok.

In a D&D campaign, Frey is worshiped by farmers, lovers, and those who seek peace and abundance. His clerics bless fields, mediate disputes, and work to foster prosperity in their communities. They celebrate the sun's warmth and the fertility of the earth through seasonal festivals. Adventurers who follow Frey seek to bring peace and plenty wherever they go, defending farming communities and resolving conflicts through diplomacy when possible.

title: Freya
summary: Norse goddess of fertility and love, the beautiful Vanir queen who chooses half the slain warriors for her hall Folkvangr.
content:
## Freya

**Title:** Lady of the Vanir, Goddess of Love
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Life
**Symbol:** Falcon
**Pantheon:** Norse

Freya is the Norse goddess of love, beauty, fertility, and war. She is the most renowned of the Vanir, riding a chariot pulled by cats and wearing the magical necklace Brisingamen. Freya has the right to claim half of all warriors slain in battle for her hall Folkvangr, with the other half going to Odin's Valhalla. She is also the foremost practitioner of seidr, a form of Norse magic associated with prophecy and fate.

In a D&D campaign, Freya is worshiped by lovers, warriors, and practitioners of divination magic. Her clerics serve as counselors in matters of love and war, and they practice prophetic arts to guide their communities. They honor both the tender and fierce aspects of their goddess. Adventurers who follow Freya are often fierce fighters who also possess a deep appreciation for beauty, love, and the mystical arts.

title: Frigga
summary: Norse goddess of birth and fertility, the wise queen of Asgard who knows the fate of all beings but speaks it to none.
content:
## Frigga

**Title:** Queen of the Aesir, Mother of All
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Life, Light
**Symbol:** Cat
**Pantheon:** Norse

Frigga is the Norse goddess of motherhood, fertility, and household management, and the queen of the Aesir as wife of Odin. She sits upon the throne Hlidskjalf alongside Odin, from which she can see all that occurs across the Nine Worlds. Frigga knows the fate of every being but refuses to speak of what she sees, carrying the burden of foreknowledge in silence. She is the patron of mothers, weavers, and those who keep the home.

In a D&D campaign, Frigga is worshiped by mothers, midwives, and those who value domestic harmony and foresight. Her clerics serve as counselors, midwives, and protectors of the home and family. They practice divination but are cautious about revealing what they learn, believing that some knowledge is too dangerous to share freely. Adventurers who follow Frigga are wise and patient, often serving as the voice of reason and the heart of their adventuring party.

title: Heimdall
summary: Norse god of watchfulness and loyalty, the ever-vigilant guardian of the Bifrost bridge whose horn will signal Ragnarok.
content:
## Heimdall

**Title:** The Watchman of the Gods, Guardian of the Bifrost
**Alignment:** Lawful Good
**Domains:** Light, War
**Symbol:** Curling musical horn
**Pantheon:** Norse

Heimdall is the Norse god of vigilance, loyalty, and protection. He stands eternal guard at the Bifrost, the rainbow bridge connecting Midgard to Asgard, watching for threats with eyes that can see a hundred leagues and ears that can hear grass growing. Heimdall requires less sleep than a bird and will blow his great horn Gjallarhorn to signal the onset of Ragnarok. He is destined to slay and be slain by Loki at the end of days.

In a D&D campaign, Heimdall is worshiped by guards, sentinels, and those who value duty and vigilance above all else. His clerics serve as watchmen, gatekeepers, and protectors of boundaries between realms. They never abandon their posts and are known for their unwavering dedication to their duties. Adventurers who follow Heimdall are vigilant guardians who protect their companions and remain ever watchful against treachery and surprise attacks.

title: Hel
summary: Norse goddess of the underworld, the half-living, half-dead ruler of Niflheim who receives those who die of sickness and old age.
content:
## Hel

**Title:** Queen of the Dead, Ruler of Niflheim
**Alignment:** Neutral Evil
**Domains:** Death
**Symbol:** Woman's face, rotting on one side
**Pantheon:** Norse

Hel is the Norse goddess of the dead and ruler of Niflheim, the cold, dark realm that receives those who die of sickness, old age, or other inglorious deaths. Daughter of Loki and the giantess Angrboda, Hel appears as a woman whose body is half beautiful and half decayed. She is a grim and unyielding ruler who hoards the souls of the dead jealously and rarely releases anyone from her realm.

In a D&D campaign, Hel is worshiped by necromancers, those who fear death, and those who deal with the restless dead. Her clerics maintain dark temples and perform rites to ease the passage of souls to Niflheim. They believe that death comes for all and that Hel's cold embrace is the ultimate fate of those who do not die gloriously in battle. Adventurers who follow Hel may seek power over death or work to ensure that souls reach their proper resting place.

metadata_json.symbol: Woman's face, rotting on one side

title: Hermod
summary: Norse god of luck, the bold messenger who rode to Hel's realm on Sleipnir to bargain for Balder's return.
content:
## Hermod

**Title:** The Brave, Messenger of the Gods
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Trickery
**Symbol:** Winged scroll
**Pantheon:** Norse

Hermod is the Norse god of luck and bold action. He is best known for his daring ride to Niflheim on Odin's eight-legged horse Sleipnir to negotiate with Hel for the return of Balder. Though his mission ultimately failed due to Loki's interference, Hermod's courage in facing death itself earned him legendary status. He embodies the Norse ideal of bold action in the face of impossible odds, trusting to luck and daring.

In a D&D campaign, Hermod is worshiped by gamblers, messengers, and those who rely on luck and audacity. His clerics serve as couriers and risk-takers, believing that fortune favors the bold. They encourage their followers to take chances and trust in luck rather than careful planning. Adventurers who follow Hermod are daring individuals who leap before they look and trust that their luck will see them through any danger.

title: Loki
summary: Norse god of thieves and trickery, the shapeshifting deceiver whose schemes bring both gifts and catastrophe to the gods.
content:
## Loki

**Title:** The Trickster, The Shapeshifter
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil
**Domains:** Trickery
**Symbol:** Flame
**Pantheon:** Norse

Loki is the Norse god of mischief, trickery, and deception. A shapeshifter and master manipulator, Loki is both an ally and enemy of the Aesir — his clever schemes have saved the gods on many occasions, but his malice has also brought them their greatest sorrows, including the death of Balder. Loki's children include the monstrous wolf Fenrir, the world-serpent Jormungandr, and the death goddess Hel. At Ragnarok, Loki will lead the forces of chaos against the gods.

In a D&D campaign, Loki is worshiped by thieves, spies, and those who thrive on deception and chaos. His clerics are master manipulators who sow discord and exploit the weaknesses of others. They view trickery as the highest art and believe that those who are deceived deserve their fate. Adventurers who follow Loki are rare but dangerous — charming, clever, and utterly untrustworthy, they advance their own agendas through lies and misdirection.

title: Njord
summary: Norse god of the sea and wind, the wealthy Vanir lord who calms storms and blesses voyages with fair weather.
content:
## Njord

**Title:** God of the Sea and Wind
**Alignment:** Neutral Good
**Domains:** Nature, Tempest
**Symbol:** Gold coin
**Pantheon:** Norse

Njord is the Norse god of the sea, wind, fishing, and wealth. He is one of the Vanir gods who came to dwell among the Aesir as part of the peace settlement between the two groups of gods. Njord lives in Noatun, his hall by the sea, and he is the father of Frey and Freya. He is a benevolent deity who calms storms, ensures good catches for fishermen, and grants prosperity to those who honor him with offerings.

In a D&D campaign, Njord is worshiped by sailors, fishermen, and merchants who trade by sea. His clerics bless ships, predict weather, and maintain harbor shrines. They serve as mediators in trade disputes and ensure that the bounty of the sea is shared fairly. Adventurers who follow Njord are drawn to sea voyages, coastal defense, and quests involving maritime commerce and exploration.

title: Odur
summary: Norse god of light and the sun, the wandering husband of Freya whose absence causes her to weep tears of gold.
content:
## Odur

**Title:** God of Light, The Wanderer
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Light
**Symbol:** Solar disk
**Pantheon:** Norse

Odur is the Norse god of light, the sun, and summer warmth. He is the husband of Freya, though he is known as an eternal wanderer who often leaves Asgard to roam the worlds, causing Freya to weep golden tears in his absence. Odur represents the warmth and light of the sun, the joy of summer days, and the restless spirit of those who cannot stay in one place. His comings and goings mirror the lengthening and shortening of days through the seasons.

In a D&D campaign, Odur is worshiped by travelers, explorers, and those who follow the sun. His clerics are wandering priests who never settle in one place for long, carrying the light of their god to distant lands. They bless travelers and illuminate dark places, both literally and figuratively. Adventurers who follow Odur are consummate wanderers driven to explore new horizons and bring light to the unexplored corners of the world.

title: Sif
summary: Norse goddess of war and earth, Thor's golden-haired wife whose strength in battle matches her connection to the harvest.
content:
## Sif

**Title:** The Golden-Haired, Lady of War
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** Upraised sword
**Pantheon:** Norse

Sif is the Norse goddess of war and the earth, and the wife of Thor. She is famed for her magnificent golden hair, which Loki once cut off as a prank and was forced to replace with hair spun by dwarven smiths from actual gold. Sif represents the fierce warrior spirit combined with the nurturing power of the earth — she is both a battle maiden and a goddess of the harvest, embodying the Norse ideal that strength and cultivation go hand in hand.

In a D&D campaign, Sif is worshiped by warriors, farmers, and those who defend their homelands with sword in hand. Her clerics serve as both war priests and harvest guardians, fighting fiercely to protect the communities they tend. They believe that a warrior's duty is to defend the land and the people who work it. Adventurers who follow Sif are stalwart defenders who combine martial skill with a deep connection to the earth.

title: Skadi
summary: Norse goddess of earth and mountains, the giant-born huntress who rules over winter's harshest peaks.
content:
## Skadi

**Title:** The Mountain Goddess, Lady of Winter
**Alignment:** Neutral
**Domains:** Nature
**Symbol:** Mountain peak
**Pantheon:** Norse

Skadi is the Norse goddess of winter, mountains, skiing, and hunting. A giantess who married into the Aesir, she chose the mountains over the sea when forced to split her time between her hall of Thrymheim and Njord's coastal home of Noatun. Skadi is a fierce and independent goddess who embodies the harsh beauty and deadly challenge of winter mountains. She hunts with bow and ski through the frozen peaks, testing the endurance of any who dare enter her domain.

In a D&D campaign, Skadi is worshiped by hunters, mountaineers, and those who thrive in cold, harsh environments. Her clerics maintain shrines on mountain peaks and serve as guides through treacherous winter terrain. They teach survival skills and respect for the unforgiving power of winter. Adventurers who follow Skadi are hardened wilderness survivors who embrace the challenge of extreme environments and test themselves against nature's harshest conditions.

title: Surtur
summary: Norse god of fire giants and war, the flame-wreathed colossus who will set the world ablaze at Ragnarok.
content:
## Surtur

**Title:** Lord of the Fire Giants, The Black One
**Alignment:** Lawful Evil
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** Flaming sword
**Pantheon:** Norse

Surtur is the Norse god of fire giants and destructive warfare. He rules Muspelheim, the realm of fire, and wields a sword that blazes brighter than the sun. At Ragnarok, Surtur will lead the fire giants across the Bifrost, shattering it with their passage, and he will set the entire world ablaze with his flaming sword, consuming everything in an inferno of destruction. He is patient, implacable, and utterly committed to the world's fiery end.

In a D&D campaign, Surtur is worshiped by fire giants, doomsday cultists, and those who seek power through destruction. His clerics wield fire as both weapon and sacrament, building forges that double as altars and burning offerings in massive bonfires. They believe that the current world must be destroyed to make way for something new. Adventurers rarely follow Surtur, but his cultists and fire giant servants make formidable antagonists in campaigns involving elemental threats.

title: Thor
summary: Norse god of storms and thunder, the mighty wielder of Mjolnir who is humanity's greatest champion against giants and monsters.
content:
## Thor

**Title:** The Thunderer, Champion of Midgard
**Alignment:** Chaotic Good
**Domains:** Tempest, War
**Symbol:** Hammer
**Pantheon:** Norse

Thor is the Norse god of thunder, storms, and the protection of humanity. Son of Odin, he is the strongest of the Aesir and wields the legendary hammer Mjolnir, which returns to his hand after being thrown and can level mountains with a single blow. Thor wears the belt Megingjord, which doubles his already immense strength, and iron gauntlets to grip his hammer. He rides across the sky in a chariot pulled by two goats, and his passage creates thunder and lightning.

In a D&D campaign, Thor is the most popular deity among Norse-inspired warriors, barbarians, and common folk. His clerics are battle-priests who charge into combat with the same reckless courage as their patron, and they serve as champions of ordinary people against supernatural threats. Adventurers who follow Thor are boisterous, brave, and fiercely loyal, always ready to stand between the innocent and the monsters that threaten them.

title: Thrym
summary: Norse god of frost giants and cold, the brutish king of Jotunheim who once stole Mjolnir to ransom it for Freya's hand.
content:
## Thrym

**Title:** Lord of the Frost Giants, King of Jotunheim
**Alignment:** Chaotic Evil
**Domains:** War
**Symbol:** White double-bladed axe
**Pantheon:** Norse

Thrym is the Norse god of frost giants and the killing cold. He is the king of the frost giants in Jotunheim, ruling through brute strength and fear. Thrym once stole Thor's hammer Mjolnir and demanded Freya as his bride in ransom — a scheme that ended in his death when Thor disguised himself as the bride and reclaimed his weapon. Thrym embodies the raw, destructive power of cold and the relentless hostility of giantkind toward the gods and mortals.

In a D&D campaign, Thrym is worshiped by frost giants, ice-dwelling creatures, and those who seek to overthrow the established order through brute force. His clerics command the power of cold and ice, building fortress-temples in the frozen wastes. They believe that might makes right and that the strong should take what they want. Adventurers rarely follow Thrym, but his frost giant armies and servants are classic antagonists in cold-climate campaigns.

title: Tyr
summary: Norse god of courage and strategy, the one-handed war god who sacrificed his hand to bind the great wolf Fenrir.
content:
## Tyr

**Title:** The Maimed God, Lord of Battles
**Alignment:** Lawful Neutral
**Domains:** Knowledge, War
**Symbol:** Upright sword
**Pantheon:** Norse

Tyr is the Norse god of war, courage, and justice. He is the bravest of the Aesir, known above all for sacrificing his right hand to bind the monstrous wolf Fenrir — the only god willing to place his hand in the beast's mouth as a pledge of good faith. Tyr represents the honorable, strategic aspects of warfare, emphasizing courage, sacrifice, and adherence to oaths. He was once the chief god of the Norse pantheon before being supplanted by Odin.

In a D&D campaign, Tyr is worshiped by honorable warriors, paladins, and military strategists. His clerics serve as military advisors, judges of martial disputes, and champions of just warfare. They believe that true courage lies not in reckless aggression but in the willingness to sacrifice for others. Adventurers who follow Tyr are disciplined fighters who value honor, keep their oaths, and never shy from sacrifice when duty demands it.

title: Uller
summary: Norse god of hunting and winter, the master archer and skier who presides over duels and winter survival.
content:
## Uller

**Title:** God of Winter and the Hunt
**Alignment:** Chaotic Neutral
**Domains:** Nature
**Symbol:** Longbow
**Pantheon:** Norse

Uller is the Norse god of hunting, winter, skiing, and archery. Stepson of Thor and son of Sif, he is the finest archer and skier among the gods. Uller presides over duels and oaths sworn upon his sacred ring. During the winter months when Odin is absent, Uller is said to rule in his place, governing Asgard with the stern discipline of the frozen season. He is a solitary, self-reliant deity who values independence and skill.

In a D&D campaign, Uller is worshiped by hunters, archers, and those who embrace the challenges of winter. His clerics teach archery, survival skills, and the art of tracking prey through frozen landscapes. They maintain hunting lodges as sacred spaces and oversee ritual duels. Adventurers who follow Uller are skilled marksmen and survivalists who thrive in cold climates and prove their worth through the hunt.
